title: Slippery elm gruel
subtitle: A soothing soothing herb for irritable bowel and gastritis
excerpt: Slippery elm bark powder stirred into warm water to make a gel-forming gruel. The classic North American Eclectic preparation for gastric irritation, acid reflux, and irritable bowel. No cooking needed.
sourceNotes: King's American Dispensatory (Felter & Lloyd, 1898), Ulmus fulva (slippery elm) entry: the gruel preparation, the gastric and intestinal soothing herb use. the herbal reference position paper on Ulmi rubrae cortex, the traditional use for gastric complaints.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Slippery elm bark powder stores 2 years in a sealed tin, cool and dark. Prepare each cup fresh; the soothing gel thickens quickly and does not store well once mixed.

glossaryTerms.definition: A slippery, gel-forming polysaccharide found in certain herbs (marshmallow root, slippery elm bark). When mixed with water, it forms a coating layer over inflamed mucous membranes.

content.text: Slippery elm bark (Ulmus rubra, inner bark) produces a remarkable amount of

when its powder meets warm water. One teaspoon in a warm cup turns gel-like within a minute. The drink coats the throat and stomach lining as it goes down. The Eclectic physicians rated it among the most useful digestive soothing herbs. They used it for gastritis, acid reflux, and irritable bowel. No cooking is needed.

attrs.title: Important safety notes
attrs.body:
Not medical advice. Consult a qualified herbalist or doctor for ongoing or serious symptoms.

Drug absorption. The soothing gel can coat the gut lining and slow or reduce the absorption of other oral medication taken at the same time. Always space slippery elm at least 2 hours away from any prescription drug.

Ethical sourcing. Wild slippery elm (Ulmus rubra) is over-harvested, and wild-crafted bark is not sustainable. Buy only from suppliers who can confirm the bark comes from cultivated trees, or substitute marshmallow root powder (same soothing herb action, sustainably available).

Pregnancy. No safety concerns at standard culinary soothing herb use are documented. Discuss therapeutic daily use with your midwife or herbalist.

items.prepNote: about 1 level teaspoon; look for inner bark powder from cultivated sources
warm, not boiling; about 70°C works well
optional; a little honey makes the preparation more palatable

content.text: Measure the slippery elm powder into a cup. Add a small splash of cold water first and stir to a smooth paste with no lumps (this prevents clumping when the hot water goes in). Then pour in the warm water (about 70°C, just off the boil) while stirring continuously. The mixture will thicken noticeably as it cools slightly. Stir in honey if using. The preparation should be the consistency of thin porridge. Drink within 5-10 minutes; it continues to thicken as it sits.

One cup (250 ml, containing 5 g powder) before meals or at the onset of gastric irritation, up to three times a day. Take at least 2 hours away from any prescription medication. Course: take for up to 4-6 weeks; if symptoms persist, seek medical advice. Paediatric use: not as a therapeutic preparation for children under twelve without specialist advice; it is however used in paediatric practice at specific doses under herbalist guidance.
Prepare each cup fresh. Slippery elm powder stores 2 years in a sealed tin, cool, dark, and dry.
When not to use this remedy

Any prescription medication within 2 hours of taking this remedy. Persistent stomach pain, pain waking you from sleep, blood in vomit or stools, black tarry stools, or unexplained weight loss all need medical assessment before any herbal treatment.
